perturbation.original_text: You will commence the Hotel Work on or before the Construction Commencement Date or Renovation Commencement Date specified in the Addendum. You may request an extension by submitting a written request for our approval before the applicable deadline, describing the status of the project and the reason for the requested extension, and paying our then-current extension fee. We may condition our approval on an update to the Plans and Designs. Once commenced, the Hotel Work will continue uninterrupted except to the extent continuation is prevented by events of Force Majeure.
perturbation.changed_text: You will endeavor to commence the Hotel Work by the Construction Commencement Date or Renovation Commencement Date specified in the Addendum. You may request an extension by submitting a written request for our consideration before the applicable deadline, generally describing the project's status and the extension's reasons, along with paying a fee we deem appropriate at the time. We might suggest updates to the Plans and Designs. Upon starting, the Hotel Work should continue without interruptions unless Force Majeure events potentially hinder it.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce several ambiguities. 'Endeavor to commence' weakens the obligation to start on time. 'For our consideration' makes the extension approval process vague. 'Generally describing' reduces the detail required in the extension request. 'A fee we deem appropriate' makes the fee amount uncertain, and 'might suggest updates' makes plan updates discretionary. 'Should continue without interruptions unless Force Majeure events potentially hinder it' weakens the guarantee of uninterrupted work.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the franchisee's obligations, creating potential non-compliance under Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110b, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. By making key obligations vague and discretionary (e.g., 'endeavor to commence,' 'fee we deem appropriate'), the franchisor could exploit the franchisee. The franchisee might incur costs based on the understanding that certain actions are required, only to find the franchisor has significant leeway. This could constitute an unfair practice, violating Connecticut law.

Legislative intent. Universal Citation: CT Gen Stat § 42-110b. (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) No person shall engage in unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing subsection (a) of this section, the commissioner and the courts of this state shall be guided by interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts to Section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 USC 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The commissioner may, in accordance with chapter 54, establish by regulation acts, practices or methods which shall be deemed to be unfair or deceptive in violation of subsection (a) of this section. Such regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the federal trade commission and the federal courts in interpreting the provisions of the Federal Trade Commission Act. (d) It is the intention of the legislature that this chapter be remedial and be so construed. (P.A. 73-615, S. 2, 16; P.A. 75-618, S. 1, 11; P.A. 76-303, S. 1, 4.) perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110b by introducing ambiguity and weakening the franchisee's obligations, potentially leading to unfair trade practices. The original text mandated specific actions (e.g., commencing work 'on or before' a date), while the changed text uses weaker language ('endeavor to commence'), creating uncertainty. Similarly, changes related to extension requests and fees introduce discretion that could be exploited, violating the prohibition of 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices' as stated in § 42-110b(a).

perturbation.original_text: You must give written notice to us specifying the nature and duration of any event of Force Majeure promptly after becoming aware of the event, and specifying that you have used, and continue to use, reasonable endeavours to mitigate the effects of such event until such event ceases to exist.
perturbation.changed_text: You should provide some written indication of any potential Force Majeure event shortly after awareness, generally outlining its nature and expected duration. Reasonable steps to lessen the event's impact should ideally be considered.
perturbation.explanation: The revised text uses weaker language like 'should provide some written indication' instead of 'must give written notice,' making the notification optional. 'Shortly after awareness' replaces 'promptly after becoming aware,' reducing the urgency. 'Generally outlining' is less specific than 'specifying the nature and duration,' and 'should ideally be considered' is weaker than 'you have used, and continue to use, reasonable endeavours,' making mitigation discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text compromises the franchisee's ability to claim protection under 'Force Majeure,' potentially violating Connecticut General Statutes § 42-133ee, which mandates good faith and fair dealing in franchise agreements. By weakening the notice requirements and mitigation efforts, the franchisor can dispute the validity of a Force Majeure claim, claiming insufficient notice or mitigation. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith, making it more difficult for the franchisee to enforce their rights, especially if events of Force Majeure arise, since there is no clear obligation from the franchisee perspective anymore.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract by using non-compulsory language around the 'Force Majeure' event. The original contract stated that the business 'must give written notice'. The changed text uses the language 'should provide some written indication', which makes the requirement non-compulsory. The 'Law Explanation' states that Connecticut General Statutes § 42-133ee requires good faith and fair dealing in franchise agreements, and weakening notice requirements undermines the franchisee's ability to claim protection under 'Force Majeure'. The 'Scraped Law Snippet 2' discusses civil action for injunction and damages but does not have sufficient information to evaluate the contradiction. The change from a mandatory obligation to a suggestion makes the contract ambiguous and weakens the franchisee's position, creating a contradiction under the ambiguity definition.

perturbation.original_text: If you cannot begin or complete reconstruction within these time frames, you may request extension approval from us in writing, describing the status of the project and the reason for the requested extension, which we will consider in accordance with our standard business practice.
perturbation.changed_text: If initiating or finishing reconstruction within those periods proves unfeasible, submitting an extension request might be contemplated, detailing project standing and rationale. Such requests may undergo review following general business thoughts.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the original obligations. 'If initiating or finishing reconstruction within those periods proves unfeasible' softens the condition. 'Submitting an extension request might be contemplated' makes the request optional. 'Detailing project standing and rationale' is less prescriptive than 'describing the status of the project and the reason for the requested extension.' 'Such requests may undergo review following general business thoughts' is vague compared to 'which we will consider in accordance with our standard business practice,' making the evaluation process less predictable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the extension process vague and discretionary, the modified text could violate Connecticut General Statutes § 42-133bb, which prohibits a franchisor from terminating, canceling, or failing to renew a franchise without good cause. If reconstruction is delayed due to unforeseen circumstances, and the franchisor unreasonably denies an extension based on the ambiguous 'general business thoughts' review, it could be argued that they are effectively terminating the franchise without good cause, as it would be impossible for the franchisee to comply with obligations without an extension and without any clear path of obtaining said extension.

Previous Next Notwithstanding the terms, provisions or conditions of any franchise agreement or other agreement between a manufacturer or distributor and a dealer, no manufacturer or distributor shall require that a dealer: (1) Order or accept delivery of any new motor vehicle, part or accessory, equipment or any other commodity not required by law in connection with warranty service or a recall campaign or voluntarily ordered by the dealer, except that the provisions of this subdivision shall not affect terms or provisions of a franchise requiring dealers to market a representative line of motor vehicles which the manufacturer or distributor is publicly advertising; (2) Order or accept delivery of any new motor vehicle with special features, accessories or equipment not included in the list price of such motor vehicles as publicly advertised by the manufacturer or distributor; (3) Pay all or part of the cost of an advertising campaign or contest, or purchase any promotional materials, training material, showroom or other display decorations or materials at the expense of the new
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities regarding the extension process, potentially violating Connecticut General Statutes § 42-133bb. The original contract clearly states the process for requesting and considering extensions. The modified text, however, uses language like 'might be contemplated' and 'general business thoughts,' which weakens the franchisee's ability to obtain an extension, which could be argued as a constructive termination without good cause. Snippet 2 establishes that 'no manufacturer or distributor shall require that a dealer,' setting a precedent about distributors' behaviour towards dealers.
